questions.question: How many onto (or surjective) functions are there from an n-element (n >= 2) set to a 2-element set?  A. 2^n  B. 2^n - 1  C. 2^n - 2  D. 2(2^n - 2)
questions.to_find: Number of onto functions from an n-element set onto a 2-element set

understand.plain: Every element of the n-element domain may be sent to either of the 2 target values, giving 2^n functions in all. A function is onto only when both target values are actually hit. So we throw away the two functions that miss a target: the one that maps everything to the first value and the one that maps everything to the second value. That leaves 2^n - 2.

keywords.explain: Every element of the codomain is the image of at least one domain element, so nothing in the target is left unused.
keywords.example: For a 2-element target {a, b}, both a and b must be hit by some input.

keywords.explain: Count everything, then subtract the cases that fail the condition: onto = total - (functions missing a value).
keywords.example: Total 2^n functions minus the 2 constant functions = 2^n - 2.

keywords.explain: A function that sends every input to the same single output, which can never be onto a 2-element set.
keywords.example: f(x) = a for all x misses b, so it is not surjective.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='70' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl'>Domain (n)</text><text x='290' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl'>Target (2)</text><circle cx='70' cy='70' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='70' cy='120' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='70' cy='170' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>x_1 ... x_n</text><circle cx='290' cy='95' r='16' class='hi'/><circle cx='290' cy='150' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='290' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>a</text><text x='290' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>b</text><line x1='84' y1='70' x2='274' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='84' y1='120' x2='274' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='84' y1='170' x2='274' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='180' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>both a and b hit => onto</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An n-element domain mapping onto a 2-element target where both target values a and b are reached, illustrating that onto excludes only the two constant maps.

given.aim: Count the surjective functions from an n-element set onto a 2-element set.

terms.meaning: Size of the domain set, with n >= 2 so onto maps exist
terms.connects: Drives the total count 2^n via the product rule

terms.meaning: Number of target values, both of which must be hit
terms.connects: Two values lead to exactly two failing (constant) functions

terms.meaning: Functions that miss at least one target value, here the two constants
terms.connects: Subtracted from the total to leave the onto count

given.plan: Count all 2^n functions, then subtract the functions that are not onto (the two constant maps that miss a target value).

steps.title: Count all functions
steps.formula_raw: n^m with m = n, codomain size = 2
steps.apply: Each of the n domain elements independently picks one of 2 images, so the total number of functions is 2^n.
steps.note: This is the product rule applied n times.

steps.title: Subtract the non-onto functions
steps.apply: With m = n and k = 2: 2^n - C(2,1)*1^n + C(2,2)*0^n = 2^n - 2*1 + 0 = 2^n - 2. Equivalently, the only non-onto maps are the 2 constant functions, so 2^n - 2.
steps.note: The two removed functions send every input to a single value.

steps.title: Select the option
steps.apply: The onto count 2^n - 2 matches option C.
steps.note: n >= 2 guarantees this count is positive.

questions.formula_note: Total functions 2^n minus the 2 constant maps that miss a value gives the onto count 2^n - 2, which is option C.

questions.question: Let S denote the set of all functions f : {0,1}^4 -> {0,1}. Denote by N the number of functions from S to the set {0,1}. The value of log_2 log_2 N is ________.
questions.to_find: The value of log_2 log_2 N

understand.plain: First count how big S is. The domain {0,1}^4 has 2^4 = 16 inputs, and each maps to one of 2 outputs, so S has 2^16 functions. Now N counts functions from S (a set of size 2^16) into {0,1}, which is 2 raised to the size of S, i.e. 2^(2^16). Taking log base 2 twice peels off the exponents: log_2 N = 2^16, then log_2(2^16) = 16.

keywords.explain: The number of functions from a set of size m into a set of size k is k^m.
keywords.example: From a 16-element domain to {0,1} there are 2^16 functions.

keywords.explain: Stacked powers grow fast; here N = 2^(2^16), an exponent that itself is a power of 2.
keywords.example: log_2 of 2^(2^16) is 2^16, not 16, until you take log again.

keywords.explain: Applying log_2 twice undoes two layers of exponentiation: log_2 log_2 (2^(2^16)) = 16.

understand.visual_alt: The domain {0,1}^4 has 16 points, so S has 2^16 functions, and N = 2^(2^16); two log base 2 operations strip the exponents down to 16.

given.aim: Compute log_2 log_2 N where N counts functions from S into {0,1}.

terms.meaning: All 4-bit strings, the domain of each f in S
terms.connects: Its size 2^4 = 16 sets the exponent for |S|

terms.meaning: The set of all functions f : {0,1}^4 -> {0,1}
terms.connects: Its cardinality becomes the exponent for N

terms.meaning: The number of functions from S to {0,1}
terms.connects: Two applications of log_2 reduce it to 16

given.plan: Find |{0,1}^4| = 16, then |S| = 2^16, then N = 2^|S| = 2^(2^16), and apply log_2 twice.

steps.title: Size of S
steps.apply: The domain {0,1}^4 has 2^4 = 16 elements, and each maps to one of 2 values, so |S| = 2^16.
steps.note: Functions from a 16-set to a 2-set number 2^16.

steps.title: Size of N
steps.apply: N counts functions from S (size 2^16) into {0,1} (size 2), so N = 2^(2^16).
steps.note: Again k^m with k = 2 and m = |S| = 2^16.

steps.title: Apply log base 2 twice
steps.note: Each log_2 cancels one layer of base-2 exponentiation.

questions.formula_note: Counting functions twice gives N = 2^(2^16), so log_2 log_2 N strips both exponents to leave 16.

questions.question: Let X and Y be finite sets and f : X -> Y be a function. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?  A. For any subsets A and B of X, |f(A U B)| = |f(A)| + |f(B)|  B. For any subsets A and B of X, f(A intersect B) = f(A) intersect f(B)  C. For any subsets A and B of X, |f(A intersect B)| = min{|f(A)|, |f(B)|}  D. For any subsets S and T of Y, f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T)
questions.answer: D. For any subsets S and T of Y, f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T)
questions.to_find: Which image/preimage identity always holds for an arbitrary function f : X -> Y

understand.plain: Images (the forward direction) behave badly because two different inputs can collapse to the same output, so set operations are only preserved as inequalities, not equalities. Preimages (the backward direction) behave perfectly: an input x lands in S intersect T exactly when f(x) is in both S and T, which is the same as x being in both preimages. So option D is the always-true identity.

keywords.explain: The set of all outputs you get by feeding A into f; it can shrink when inputs share an output.
keywords.example: If f(1) = f(2) = a then f({1,2}) = {a} has size 1, not 2.

keywords.explain: All inputs whose output lands in S; it always distributes over union and intersection.
keywords.example: x in f^(-1)(S intersect T) <=> f(x) in S and f(x) in T.

keywords.explain: An operation commutes with intersection when it can be applied before or after intersecting with the same result.
keywords.example: f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T) holds for every f.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='80' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl'>X</text><text x='300' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl'>Y</text><ellipse cx='300' cy='90' rx='55' ry='35' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='300' cy='150' rx='55' ry='35' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='300' cy='120' rx='28' ry='22' class='hi'/><text x='262' y='75' class='sub mut'>S</text><text x='262' y='185' class='sub mut'>T</text><text x='300' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='tag'>S∩T</text><circle cx='80' cy='110' r='32' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='tag'>f^-1(S∩T)</text><line x1='112' y1='110' x2='272' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='190' y='225' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>preimage of S∩T = (preimage S)∩(preimage T)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A function f from X to Y showing that the preimage of S intersect T equals the intersection of the two preimages, while forward images do not preserve such equalities.

given.aim: Identify the one set identity that holds for every function f : X -> Y.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary (not necessarily injective) function between finite sets
terms.example: f could send two inputs to the same output
terms.connects: Non-injectivity is what breaks the forward-image options

terms.meaning: Forward image of a subset A of X
terms.connects: Collapsing outputs makes A, B, C fail

terms.meaning: Backward image of a subset S of Y
terms.example: f^(-1)({a}) = all inputs mapping to a
terms.connects: Distributes over intersection, making D true

given.plan: Use a small non-injective counterexample to knock out A, B and C, then prove D by the membership equivalence x in f^(-1)(S intersect T) <=> f(x) in S and f(x) in T.

steps.title: Break the forward-image options
steps.formula_raw: f(A intersect B) subset_eq f(A) intersect f(B)
steps.apply: Let f(1) = f(2) = a with A = {1}, B = {2}. Then A intersect B = empty so f(A intersect B) = empty, but f(A) intersect f(B) = {a}. This kills B and C, and a similar overlap makes |f(A U B)| < |f(A)| + |f(B)|, killing A.
steps.note: Forward images only satisfy subset relations, not equalities, when f is not injective.

steps.title: Prove the preimage identity (D)
steps.formula_raw: f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T)
steps.apply: x in f^(-1)(S intersect T) <=> f(x) in S intersect T <=> f(x) in S and f(x) in T <=> x in f^(-1)(S) and x in f^(-1)(T). So the two sides are equal for every f.
steps.note: Preimages always distribute over intersection (and union).

steps.title: Select the true statement
steps.apply: Only D holds for all functions, so the answer is option D.
steps.note: The backward direction is unconditionally well behaved.

solution.result: D. For any subsets S and T of Y, f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T)

questions.formula_note: Preimages distribute over intersection for every function (option D), whereas forward images fail when f maps distinct inputs to the same output.

questions.question: Consider the set of all functions f : {0,1,...,2014} -> {0,1,...,2014} such that f(f(i)) = i, for all 0 <= i <= 2014. Consider the following statements:  P. For each such function it must be the case that for every i, f(i) = i.  Q. For each such function it must be the case that for some i, f(i) = i.  R. Each function must be onto.  Which one of the following is CORRECT?  A. P, Q and R are true  B. Only Q and R are true  C. Only P and Q are true  D. Only R is true
questions.answer: B. Only Q and R are true
questions.to_find: Which of P, Q, R hold for every involution f with f(f(i)) = i on a 2015-element set

understand.plain: The condition f(f(i)) = i means f is its own inverse, an involution. Applying f twice gets you back, so f must be a bijection, hence onto (R is true). It need NOT be the identity, because f can swap two elements while fixing the rest (P is false). The domain {0,...,2014} has 2015 elements, an odd number; since f pairs elements into 2-cycles, at least one element has no partner and must be a fixed point (Q is true).

keywords.explain: A function equal to its own inverse: f(f(i)) = i for all i, so applying it twice returns the start.
keywords.example: Swapping 0 and 1 while fixing everything else is an involution.

keywords.explain: A function that is its own inverse must be one-to-one and onto, so R holds automatically.
keywords.example: Since f composed with f is the identity, f has an inverse and is onto.

keywords.explain: Involutions split the set into 2-cycles and fixed points; an odd-sized set cannot be all 2-cycles, forcing a fixed point.
keywords.example: 2015 elements means at least one i with f(i) = i.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='70' cy='80' r='20' class='hi'/><circle cx='180' cy='80' r='20' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>a</text><text x='180' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>b</text><path d='M88 72 Q125 50 162 72' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M162 88 Q125 110 88 88' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='125' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='tag'>2-cycle: f(a)=b, f(b)=a</text><circle cx='300' cy='80' r='20' class='ok' fill-opacity='0.25' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><text x='300' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>c</text><path d='M312 64 Q345 70 320 96' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='300' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>fixed point f(c)=c</text><text x='180' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='tag'>odd count => at least one fixed point</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An involution decomposed into 2-cycles and fixed points; with an odd 2015-element set at least one fixed point is forced, and the function is a bijection.

given.aim: Decide the truth of P (always identity), Q (has a fixed point), R (onto) for involutions on {0,...,2014}.

terms.meaning: An involution: f is its own inverse
terms.example: f swaps 0 and 1, fixes the rest
terms.connects: Forces f to be a bijection and rules out P
Oddness forces at least one fixed point, making Q true

terms.meaning: Claims: P all i fixed, Q some i fixed, R onto
terms.example: R is true, P is false, Q is true
terms.connects: Their joint truth picks the option

given.plan: Recognise f as an involution to get R, give a swap example to refute P, and use the odd cardinality to prove Q, then match the option.

steps.title: f is a bijection, so R is true
steps.apply: Since applying f twice returns i, f is its own inverse, hence one-to-one and onto. So R (each function must be onto) is TRUE.
steps.note: Having an inverse forces surjectivity on a finite set.

steps.title: Refute P with a swap
steps.apply: This f satisfies f(f(i)) = i but f(0) = 1 != 0, so not every i is fixed. Hence P (every i has f(i) = i) is FALSE.
steps.note: A single transposition is a valid involution that is not the identity.

steps.title: Prove Q from odd cardinality
steps.formula_raw: involution = product of 2-cycles and fixed points
steps.apply: An involution partitions the set into 2-cycles (swapped pairs) and fixed points. The set has 2015 elements (odd), and 2-cycles cover an even number of elements, so at least one element is left fixed. Hence Q (some i has f(i) = i) is TRUE.
steps.note: An odd-sized set cannot be fully paired into 2-cycles.

steps.title: Select the option
steps.apply: P is false, Q and R are true, which is 'Only Q and R are true', option B.
steps.note: The oddness of 2015 is the key that makes Q hold.

solution.result: B. Only Q and R are true

questions.formula_note: f(f(i)) = i makes f an involution, hence a bijection (R true); a swap shows P false, and the odd size 2015 forces a fixed point (Q true), giving B.

questions.question: If g(x) = 1 - x and h(x) = x/(x-1), then g(h(x))/h(g(x)) is:  A. h(x)/g(x)  B. -1/x  C. g(x)/h(x)  D. x/(1-x)^2
questions.to_find: The simplified value of the ratio g(h(x)) / h(g(x))

understand.plain: This is pure function composition followed by algebra. Substitute h(x) into g to get g(h(x)), substitute g(x) into h to get h(g(x)), then divide and simplify. The result turns out to equal -x/(x-1)^2, and checking option A shows h(x)/g(x) equals the very same thing.

keywords.explain: g(h(x)) means plug the whole expression h(x) wherever x appears in g.
Combine fractions over one denominator before dividing to avoid sign and cancellation errors.
Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal.

understand.visual_alt: Compose g with h and h with g, simplify each, then divide to get -x/(x-1)^2, which matches h(x)/g(x) in option A.

given.aim: Simplify g(h(x)) / h(g(x)) and match it to one of the options.

terms.meaning: The outer/inner map used in the composition
terms.connects: Used to build g(h(x)) and h(g(x))

terms.meaning: The rational map composed with g
terms.connects: Plugged into g and receives g as an argument

given.plan: Compute g(h(x)) and h(g(x)) by substitution, divide, simplify the rational expression, and then identify which option equals it.

steps.title: Compute g(h(x))
steps.note: Put 1 over the common denominator (x-1).

steps.title: Compute h(g(x))
steps.note: Numerator and denominator both simplified before dividing.

steps.title: Form and simplify the ratio
steps.note: Dividing by a fraction multiplies by its reciprocal.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.apply: h(x)/g(x) = x/((x-1)(1-x)) = x/(-(x-1)^2) = -x/(x-1)^2, which equals the ratio. So the answer is option A.
steps.note: Option A matches the simplified result exactly.

questions.formula_note: Composing and simplifying gives g(h(x))/h(g(x)) = -x/(x-1)^2, which is exactly h(x)/g(x), so the answer is A.

questions.question: The number of onto functions (surjective functions) from set X = {1,2,3,4} to set Y = {a,b,c} is ________.
questions.to_find: Number of onto functions from a 4-element set onto a 3-element set

understand.plain: We want functions from 4 inputs onto 3 outputs that hit every one of a, b, c. There are 3^4 = 81 functions total, but many miss a target value. Inclusion-exclusion removes the maps that skip at least one value: subtract the 3 ways to drop a value (each leaving 2^4 maps) and add back the 3 ways to drop two values (each leaving 1^4). That gives 81 - 48 + 3 = 36.

keywords.explain: Onto functions from m elements onto k = sum_{i=0}^{k} (-1)^i C(k,i) (k-i)^m.
Subtract functions missing one value, add back those missing two, and so on, to avoid double counting.
keywords.example: Three single-value omissions, three double-value omissions for k=3.

keywords.explain: Onto count also equals k! * S(m, k), where S(4,3) = 6 partitions of the inputs into 3 nonempty blocks.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='75' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl'>X (4)</text><text x='285' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl'>Y (3)</text><circle cx='75' cy='55' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='75' cy='95' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='75' cy='135' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='75' cy='175' r='13' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='120' class='sub mut'>1..4</text><circle cx='285' cy='70' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='285' cy='120' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='285' cy='170' r='15' class='hi'/><text x='285' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>a</text><text x='285' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>b</text><text x='285' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub'>c</text><line x1='88' y1='55' x2='270' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='88' y1='95' x2='270' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='88' y1='135' x2='270' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='88' y1='175' x2='270' y2='170' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='180' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>all of a,b,c hit => 36 onto maps</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 4-element domain mapping onto a 3-element target where each of a, b, c is hit, counted by inclusion-exclusion to give 36.

given.aim: Count surjective functions from X (4 elements) onto Y (3 elements).

terms.example: Each of 1,2,3,4 picks an image in Y

terms.meaning: The 3-element codomain, k = 3, all of which must be hit
terms.example: An onto map uses a, b and c

terms.meaning: Target elements left unused by a non-onto map
terms.example: Maps avoiding a use only {b,c}, giving 2^4 each
terms.connects: Subtracted and added back by inclusion-exclusion

given.plan: Apply the surjective-count formula sum_{i=0}^{3} (-1)^i C(3,i)(3-i)^4, or equivalently 3! * S(4,3).

steps.title: Write the inclusion-exclusion sum
steps.note: Terms alternate sign as we exclude more target values.

steps.title: Evaluate each term
steps.note: C(3,1)=3 and C(3,2)=3 by symmetry.

steps.title: Cross-check with Stirling numbers
steps.apply: S(4,3) = 6 (ways to split 4 elements into 3 nonempty blocks), so 3! * 6 = 6 * 6 = 36, matching.
steps.note: Both methods give the same answer.

questions.formula_note: Inclusion-exclusion gives 3^4 - 3*2^4 + 3*1^4 = 36 onto functions, equal to 3! * S(4,3).

formulas.name: Number of Onto (Surjective) Functions
formulas.plain: Onto functions from an m-set onto a k-set = sum_{i=0}^{k} (-1)^i C(k,i) (k-i)^m = k! * S(m,k)
formulas.whenToUse: To count surjective functions from a set of size m onto a set of size k, where every codomain element must be used.

terms.means: size of the codomain (all must be hit)
Stirling number of the second kind: partitions of m elements into k nonempty blocks

formulas.trap: Onto requires m >= k, otherwise the count is 0; for k = 2 the formula collapses to 2^m - 2.

formulas.name: Preimage Distributes over Intersection
formulas.plain: f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T) for every function f
formulas.whenToUse: When relating preimages of intersected (or unioned) subsets of the codomain; holds for any function.

terms.means: preimage: all x with f(x) in S

formulas.trap: This is a property of preimages only; forward images f(A intersect B) merely satisfy a subset relation, not equality, unless f is injective.
formulas.reference: Set theory of functions / GATE Discrete Mathematics

formulas.name: Involution (Self-Inverse Function)
formulas.plain: f(f(i)) = i means f is its own inverse: a bijection made of 2-cycles and fixed points
formulas.whenToUse: When a function satisfies f composed with f equal to identity, so it is self-inverse and necessarily a bijection.

terms.means: an i with f(i) = i, not part of a swapped pair

formulas.trap: An involution on an odd-sized set must have at least one fixed point, but it need not be the identity; on an even-sized set it may have none.
formulas.reference: Permutations / group theory / GATE Discrete Mathematics

formulas.name: Function Composition
formulas.plain: (g o h)(x) = g(h(x)) — substitute h(x) into g wherever its variable appears
formulas.whenToUse: When evaluating nested functions such as g(h(x)) by substituting one function's output into another.

terms.means: the outer function applied to the inner result

formulas.trap: Composition is not commutative: g(h(x)) generally differs from h(g(x)).
